retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- History and Physical -- Ameloblastoma
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically, it appears as a small, well-circumscribed lesion (less than 3 cm) around an unerupted tooth. A calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumor (Pindborg tumor) is a rare tumor that accounts for less than 1% of the total number of odontogenic tumor cases. It mainly affects patients between the age of 30 and 50 years of age. However, fewer cases have been reported with a wider age range. [49] Mandible seems to be the most affected area, with around 66% of cases. [50]

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Histologically, it is most important to differentiate common ameloblastomas vs. ameloblastic carcinoma/malignant ameloblastoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Evaluation -- Ameloblastoma
retrieved_docs.content: Other tumors include adenomatoid odontogenic tumor that usually appears as a well-circumscribed unilocular translucency around an unerupted tooth, usually a canine. [47] [48] A calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumor (Pindborg tumor) can be either unilocular or multi-locular. It usually appears as a radiolucent area with flecks of radio-opacities with an indistinct line of demarcation from the unaffected bone. It may be associated with an impacted tooth. [55] (Fig. 4) Finally, squamous odontogenic tumors that are usually unilocular in small lesions and multilocular in large ones and they may displace the affected tooth. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Pathophysiology -- Odontogenic Tumors That Arise Only From Odontogenic Epithelium Without Odontogenic Ectomesenchyme
retrieved_docs.content: Other types in this category include calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumors (Pindborg tumor), which usually arises from the remnants of the dental lamina or reduced enamel epithelium, and squamous odontogenic tumors that usually originate from the remnants of the dental lamina. [16] The adenomatoid odontogenic tumor usually arises from odontogenic epithelium around the crowns of unerupted teeth. It is considered by many as a hamartoma. It was previously considered as a variant of ameloblastoma named adenoameloblastoma. However, a better understanding of the biological behavior of this tumor changed this concept. It is biologically non-aggressive. [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Differential Diagnosis -- Examples of These Lesions
retrieved_docs.content: Dentigerous cyst: It usually presents as a unilocular translucent lesion that causes painless bony expansion around the impacted third molar tooth. [65] Traumatic bone cyst: It usually presents as a unilocular translucent lesion affecting the mandibular molar and premolar region in young adults. It always spares the inferior alveolar canal. [66] Aneurysmal bone cyst: It mainly affects the long bones and vertebral column of young adults rather than jaws. It can either present as unilocular or multilocular translucent lesions with marked cortical expansion and teeth displacement. [67]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

Both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and the broader category Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant share the relationship termed النقيلة, suggesting a common classification or characteristic within neoplastic disease. Recognizing that the metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland represents a subset of malignant sublingual gland tumors underscores its aggressive potential and the need for thorough evaluation, while the ambiguous neoplasm category highlights the importance of histopathological assessment to determine benign versus malignant status. The linking term النقيلة therefore serves as a unifying descriptor that aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing these related neoplastic conditions.

Cancer of bone and connective tissue is considered equivalent to malignant neoplasm of the temporal bone and is mapped to the Arabic term ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي (malignant tumor of the upper jaw); the Arabic phrase سرطان العظم (bone cancer) is classified under ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي, indicating that bone cancer is regarded as a type of malignant upper‑jaw tumor. Collectively, these cross‑lingual and anatomical mappings illustrate how metastatic skin lesions, facial skin tumors of uncertain behavior, and malignant bone neoplasms of the skull, facial bones, and upper jaw are interrelated within broader categories of metastatic disease, head‑and‑neck oncology, and bone‑and‑connective‑tissue cancers, underscoring the need for integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head refers to any cancer that has spread to structures within the head region, and within this broader category the specific condition of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head represents a more precise manifestation. Recognizing that metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head is a subset of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head helps clinicians assess the extent of disease spread, determine appropriate staging, and tailor management strategies for patients with head‑region metastases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 1118
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to frontal bone, metastatic malignant neoplasm to occipital bone, and metastatic malignant neoplasm to temporal bone are each classified as malignant neoplasms of their respective bones—malignant neoplasm of frontal bone, malignant neoplasm of occipital bone, and malignant neoplasm of temporal bone (diagnosis). The broader diagnostic category cancer of bone and connective tissue is mapped to each of these specific bone‑site malignant neoplasms, indicating that they are considered subtypes within the general spectrum of bone and connective‑tissue cancers. In addition, cancer of bone and connective tissue is also mapped to the Arabic term “ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي,” which denotes a malignant tumor of the upper jaw, reflecting cross‑lingual terminology alignment. Together, these relationships illustrate how metastatic bone cancers are organized under a unified cancer of bone and connective tissue classification while also being linked to equivalent expressions in other medical vocabularies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of the disease سرطانة العظم. In patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of metastatic lesions indicates that malignant cells have spread to bone from another primary site, while secondary lesions represent additional tumor deposits that develop as a consequence of the primary bone malignancy. Recognizing these lesions is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and the formulation of appropriate treatment strategies for سرطانة العظم.
